abstract:
This paper addresses the challenge of enhancing the battery life and computational power of mobile devices through task ofﬂoading in a multicell MIMO environment. We consider a scenario where multiple mobile users (MUs) compete for the computational resources of a common cloud server while experiencing intercell interference. The problem is framed as a joint optimization of radio resources—specifically the transmit precoding matrices of the MUs—and computational resources—the CPU cycle rate allocated by the cloud—to minimize total energy consumption subject to strict latency constraints. 

The resulting optimization problem is non-convex and non-separable due to the coupling of interference and latency requirements. For the single-user case, we demonstrate that the problem can be transformed into a convex form, allowing for a global optimal solution in closed form. For the more complex multiuser setting, we develop an iterative algorithmic framework based on successive convex approximation (SCA) that is guaranteed to converge to a local optimum. To facilitate practical deployment, we further derive distributed and parallel versions of the algorithm that require minimal coordination between the cloud and the radio access points. Numerical simulations validate our approach, demonstrating that the proposed joint optimization significantly outperforms conventional disjoint resource allocation schemes, particularly for computation-intensive applications.
